rescue_reasoning: Although the sub-statement does not explicitly include an outcome token like 'reduce emissions', its parent statement explicitly establishes the mitigation objective: 'retrofit existing buildings to reduce emissions from energy use in buildings.' The sub-statement ('Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote building retrofits...') is a direct policy instrument to drive those retrofits, aligning with mitigation via reduced building energy use and emissions. Climate screen is 'explicit_parent', so it should be retained as Mitigation.
policy_statement: Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote building retrofits, starting with LMI buildings.
mechanism_description: Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives to promote building retrofits, reducing building energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building operation + retrofit investment → ↓ energy demand → ↓ fuel combustion (onsite + grid) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building owners/managers undertake energy-efficiency retrofits (e.g., insulation, air sealing, high-efficiency HVAC, lighting, controls) because incentives lower upfront cost/friction and improve project payback.  
2) Mediating process: Improved thermal performance and equipment efficiency reduce heat loss/gain and increase conversion efficiency, lowering delivered electricity and fuel needed to provide the same indoor comfort/services.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced energy generation/combustion lowers CO₂ (and associated CH₄/N₂O and criteria pollutants) emissions; magnitude depends on fuel mix and measure set.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption, which reduces fossil fuel use in heating/electricity supply and therefore lowers GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Incentives → more/ deeper retrofits → lower energy use → lower fossil fuel combustion → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While retrofits can also improve comfort/heat resilience and reduce energy bills (resource efficiency co-benefit), the mechanism’s central climate impact across instances is emissions reduction from reduced energy demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: If incentives are primarily for resilience-only upgrades (e.g., floodproofing) the mechanism would differ; if retrofits include renewables, that is a different/additional mechanism, but the stated canonical mechanism is energy-use reduction via retrofits.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits “building retrofit incentives → energy use reduction” will, by design, primarily reduce operational energy demand and thus emissions, regardless of city/context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy should be tagged with additional subtypes (e.g., electrification, renewable integration, equity/LMI targeting, adaptation co-benefits) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Retrofits may sometimes increase electricity use if they shift from gas to electric (electrification) or add cooling; net mitigation still typically holds (especially with cleaner grids) but the dominant effect can vary by grid carbon intensity and measure mix. If incentives are dominated by non-energy upgrades or renewable-only installations, reclassification to a different mechanism/category may be warranted.
key_indicators: "Offer monetary and/or non-monetary incentives"; "promote building retrofits"; "starting with LMI buildings"
co_benefits: Energy cost savings/affordability for LMI households; improved indoor comfort/health; potential local air quality improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Non-monetary incentives could include technical assistance or expedited permitting, which can look like enabling support; however, the instrument is still best classified as an incentive-program because the central lever is “offer incentives” to trigger retrofit uptake.
